entries.name: Smith Plot
entries.description: Takes a picture and the data for a Smith plot and appends a picture that represents the Smith plot of the data. Functions © National Instruments 2003

terminals.name: [user-specified font]
terminals.description: [user-specified font] specifies the specific font characteristics for the text to draw. The VI ignores this input unless desired font is User-specified Font.

terminals.name: Font Name:
terminals.description: Font Name specifies the font to use for the text. If you misspell the font name or specify a font that is not installed on the computer, the operating system selects a font.

terminals.name: Size
terminals.description: Size specifies the size of the font in points.

terminals.name: Strikeout?
terminals.description: Strikeout? specifies if the text appears in strikeout. If Strikeout? is TRUE, the text appears in strikeout.

terminals.name: Italic?
terminals.description: Italic? specifies if the text appears in italics. If Italic? is TRUE, the text appears in italics.

terminals.name: Underline?
terminals.description: Underline? specifies if the text is underlined. If Underline? is TRUE, the text appears underlined.

terminals.name: Outline?
terminals.description: Outline? specifies if the text is outlined. If Outline? is TRUE, the text appears outlined.

terminals.name: Shadow?
terminals.description: Shadow? specifies if the text has a shadow. If Shadow? is TRUE, the text appears in shadow.

terminals.name: Bold?
terminals.description: Functions 2004 ni.com Bold? specifies if the text is bold. If Bold? is TRUE, the text appears bold.

terminals.name: desired font(Application)
terminals.description: desired font specifies the text font. 0 User-specified Font 1 Application Font (default) 2 System Font 3 Dialog Font

terminals.name: picture
terminals.description: picture is the picture to which you want to add the plot. The default is an empty picture.

terminals.name: smith plot
terminals.description: smith plot is an array of points, where each point is a cluster containing the r and x values that make up the complex impedance, z, of r + jx.

terminals.name: dimensions (w,h)
terminals.description: dimensions specifies the top left point of the drawn bitmap.

terminals.name: width
terminals.description: width specifies the horizontal coordinate that increases to the right.

terminals.description: Zo is the characteristic impedance of the system.

terminals.name: points {r,x}
terminals.description: points {r,x} is an array of points, where each point is a cluster containing the r and x values that make up the complex impedance r + jx.

terminals.name: points complex
terminals.description: points complex is an array of complex numbers.

terminals.name: normalized smith points {r,x}
terminals.description: Functions 2014 ni.com normalized smith points {r,x} is an array of clusters of points containing the normalized data. Each cluster contains an r and an x value.

terminals.name: scale definition
terminals.description: scale definition specifies the pixel coordinates and values for both ends of the scale, and the numeric format and precision for the scale numbers.

terminals.name: coordinate 1
terminals.description: coordinate 1 contains the pixel coordinates for the left of a vertical scale or the top of a horizontal scale.

terminals.name: value 1
terminals.description: value 1 contains the minimum value for the scale numbers.

terminals.name: coordinate 2
terminals.description: coordinate 2 contains the pixel coordinates for the right of a vertical scale or the bottom of a horizontal scale.

terminals.name: value 2
terminals.description: value 2 contains the maximum value for the scale numbers.

terminals.name: log?(F)
terminals.description: log? specifies if the scale is logarithmic.

terminals.name: fmt and prec
terminals.description: fmt and prec specifies the numeric format and precision for the scale numbers.

terminals.name: format
terminals.description: format specifies the numeric format for the scale numbers. Functions © National Instruments 2047

terminals.name: picture
terminals.description: picture is the picture to which you want to add the scale. The default is an empty picture.

terminals.name: scale specs
terminals.description: scale specs is an array of clusters containing the coordinates and labels you can use in the scale. Use the Calc Scale Specs VI to obtain the values for this input.

terminals.name: coordinate
terminals.description: coordinate specifies the coordinates to use in the scale.

terminals.name: label
terminals.description: label specifies the labels to use in the scale.

terminals.name: constant coordinate
terminals.description: constant coordinate is the location of the x coordinate in a vertical scale, or the y coordinate in a horizontal scale.

terminals.name: tick length(3)
terminals.description: tick length specifies the length of the tick marks on the edge of the scale.

terminals.name: scale attributes

entries.name: Map Setup
entries.description: Uses pixel and data ranges to determine scale factors such that (a * data value) + b is the pixel value that corresponds to that data value.

terminals.name: log?(F)
terminals.description: log? specifies if the scale is logarithmic.

terminals.name: coordinate 1
terminals.description: coordinate 1 is the pixel location for the first point.

terminals.name: value 1
terminals.description: value 1 is the actual data value for the first point.

terminals.name: coordinate 2
terminals.description: coordinate 2 is the pixel location for the second point.

terminals.name: value 2
terminals.description: value 2 is the actual data value for the second point.

terminals.name: scale factors
terminals.description: scale factors is the scale factor created from the two points. Use this output as the input for other VIs such as the Map Pixel to Value VI and the Map Value to Pixel VI. Functions

entries.name: Map Value to Pixel
entries.description: Uses the domain value and the cluster returned by the Map Setup VI to determine a pixel value corresponding to that domain value.

terminals.name: scale factors
terminals.description: scale factors is a cluster returned by the Map Setup VI.

terminals.name: log?(F)
terminals.description: log? specifies if the scale is logarithmic. Functions 2054 ni.com

terminals.name: pixel
terminals.description: pixel is the pixel value corresponding to the domain value.

entries.name: Map Pixel to Value
entries.description: Uses the pixel value and the cluster returned by the Map Setup VI to determine the domain value corresponding to that pixel value.

terminals.name: scale factors
terminals.description: scale factors is a cluster returned by the Map Setup VI.

terminals.name: log?(F)
terminals.description: log? specifies if the scale is logarithmic.

terminals.name: pixel
terminals.description: pixel is the pixel value.

entries.name: Picture Functions
entries.description: Use the Picture Functions VIs to create visual representations of data. Many of these picture VIs use clusters to define points and rectangles. The VIs related to drawing text use clusters and enumerations to describe the font choice and the positioning of the text. Palette Object Description Draw Point Sets a pixel in a picture to a specified color. Move Pen Changes the current pen location of a picture to the specified position or by the specified amount if the position is relative. Draw Line

entries.name: Draw Point
entries.description: Sets a pixel in a picture to a specified color. Functions 2058 ni.com

terminals.name: picture
terminals.description: picture is the picture to which you want to add a pixel. The default is an empty picture.

terminals.name: point(x,y)
terminals.description: point(x,y) is the coordinate of the pixel to set in the resulting picture relative to the current location of the origin.

terminals.name: color(black)
terminals.description: color is the color of the pixel. The default is black. You can wire a color box constant to this input.

terminals.name: pen
terminals.description: pen sets the width and style of the pen the VI uses to draw the picture.

terminals.name: Width
terminals.description: Width sets the width in pixels of the pen the VI uses to draw the picture.

terminals.name: Style
terminals.description: Style sets the style of the line the VI uses to draw the picture. Functions

entries.name: Move Pen
entries.description: Changes the current pen location of a picture to the specified position or by the specified amount if the position is relative.

terminals.name: picture
terminals.description: picture is the picture whose current pen location you want to move. The default is an empty picture.

terminals.name: new position
terminals.description: new position is the point to which the pen moves in the new picture. Functions 2060 ni.com If absolute coordinates? is TRUE, the pen moves to the absolute coordinate specified by new position. If absolute coordinates? is FALSE, the pen moves relative to the current pen location in picture.

terminals.name: absolute coordinates?(T)
terminals.description: absolute coordinates? specifies whether the point new position is in absolute or relative coordinates. The default is absolute (TRUE).

terminals.name: new picture
terminals.description: new picture is the picture that contains the new image. You can wire this output to any other picture input to add more drawing instructions to the picture. You also can wire this output to the Picture to Pixmap VI to obtain an image data cluster. You then can use the Graphics Formats VIs to save the image data to a file. You can wire this output to the Concatenate Strings function to concatenate the output from multiple Picture Functions VIs so that they draw on a single picture control. Exampl

entries.name: Draw Line
entries.description: Draws a line from the current pen position to the specified location in a picture. Functions © National Instruments 2061 The end point is either absolute or relative to the current position, depending on the value of the absolute coordinates? input.

terminals.name: picture
terminals.description: picture is the picture to which you want to add the line. The default is an empty picture.

terminals.name: end point
terminals.description: end point is the end point of the new line. If absolute coordinates? is TRUE, the line draws to the absolute coordinate that end point specifies. If absolute coordinates? is FALSE, the line draws relative to the current pen location in picture.

terminals.name: absolute coordinates?(T)
terminals.description: absolute coordinates? specifies whether the point new position is in absolute or relative coordinates. The default is absolute (TRUE).

terminals.name: color(black)
terminals.description: color is the color of the pixel. The default is black. Functions 2062 ni.com You can wire a color box constant to this input.

terminals.name: pen
terminals.description: pen sets the width and style of the pen the VI uses to draw the picture.

terminals.name: Width
terminals.description: Width sets the width in pixels of the pen the VI uses to draw the picture.

terminals.name: Style
terminals.description: Style sets the style of the line the VI uses to draw the picture. 0 Solid (default) 1 Dash 2 Dot 3 DashDot 4 DashDotDot

terminals.name: new picture
terminals.description: new picture is the picture that contains the new image. You can wire this output to any other picture input to add more drawing instructions to the picture. You also can wire this output to the Picture to Pixmap VI to obtain an image data cluster. You then can use the Graphics Formats VIs to save the image data to a file. You can wire this output to the Concatenate Strings function to concatenate the output from multiple Picture Functions VIs so that they draw on a single picture control. Exampl

entries.name: Draw Multiple Lines
entries.description: Draws multiple connected lines into a picture. You also can use the VI to draw color-filled polygons by passing TRUE to the fill? input. All points are absolute coordinates.

terminals.name: picture
terminals.description: picture is the picture to which you want to add lines. The default is an empty picture.

terminals.name: line endpoints
terminals.description: line endpoints is an array of points describing the lines to draw. The lines are drawn in the specified color, connecting the points in order. All coordinates are absolute.

terminals.name: fill?(F)
terminals.description: fill? specifies whether to fill the interior. If FALSE (default), the VI draws only the outlines.

terminals.name: color(black)
terminals.description: Functions 2064 ni.com color is the color of the lines. If the interior is filled, the VI uses this color for the interior. The default is black. You can wire a color box constant to this input.

terminals.name: pen
terminals.description: pen sets the width and style of the pen the VI uses to draw the picture.

terminals.name: Width
terminals.description: Width sets the width in pixels of the pen the VI uses to draw the picture.

terminals.name: Style
terminals.description: Style sets the style of the line the VI uses to draw the picture. 0 Solid (default) 1 Dash 2 Dot 3 DashDot 4 DashDotDot

terminals.name: new picture
terminals.description: new picture is the picture that contains the new image. You can wire this output to any other picture input to add more drawing instructions to the picture. You also can wire this output to the Picture to Pixmap VI to obtain an image data cluster. You then can use the Graphics Formats VIs to save the image data to a file. You can wire this output to the Concatenate Strings function to concatenate the output from multiple Picture Functions VIs so that they draw on a single picture control.

entries.name: Draw Rectangle
entries.description: Draws a rectangle, optionally filling the interior. Functions © National Instruments 2065

terminals.name: picture
terminals.description: picture is the picture to which you want to add the rectangle. The default is an empty picture.

terminals.name: rect
terminals.description: rect is a cluster that contains four numeric controls that describe the left, top, right, and bottom coordinates of the outer edges of the rectangle. Horizontal coordinates increase to the right, and vertical coordinates increase to the bottom.

terminals.name: left
terminals.description: left is the horizontal coordinate of the left edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: top
terminals.description: top is the vertical coordinate of the top edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: right
terminals.description: right is the horizontal coordinate of the right edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: bottom
terminals.description: bottom is the vertical coordinate of the bottom edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: fill?(F)
terminals.description: fill? specifies whether to fill the interior. If FALSE (default), the VI draws only the outlines.

terminals.name: color(black)
terminals.description: color is the color of the lines. Functions 2066 ni.com If the interior is filled, the VI uses this color for the interior. The default is black. You can wire a color box constant to this input.

terminals.name: pen
terminals.description: pen sets the width and style of the pen the VI uses to draw the picture.

terminals.name: Width
terminals.description: Width sets the width in pixels of the pen the VI uses to draw the picture.

terminals.name: Style
terminals.description: Style sets the style of the line the VI uses to draw the picture. 0 Solid (default) 1 Dash 2 Dot 3 DashDot 4 DashDotDot

terminals.name: new picture
terminals.description: new picture is the picture that contains the new image. You can wire this output to any other picture input to add more drawing instructions to the picture. You also can wire this output to the Picture to Pixmap VI to obtain an image data cluster. You then can use the Graphics Formats VIs to save the image data to a file. You can wire this output to the Concatenate Strings function to concatenate the output from multiple Picture Functions VIs so that they draw on a single picture control.

entries.name: Draw Grayed Out Rect
entries.description: Draws a patterned rectangle in the specified picture to create the effect of graying-out a section of the picture. An underlying picture shows through the holes in the pattern. The VI does not frame Functions © National Instruments 2067 the rectangle.

terminals.name: picture
terminals.description: picture is the picture to which you want to add the rectangle. The default is an empty picture.

terminals.name: rect
terminals.description: rect is a cluster that contains four numeric controls that describe the left, top, right, and bottom coordinates of the outer edges of the rectangle. Horizontal coordinates increase to the right, and vertical coordinates increase to the bottom.

terminals.name: left
terminals.description: left is the horizontal coordinate of the left edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: top
terminals.description: top is the vertical coordinate of the top edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: right
terminals.description: right is the horizontal coordinate of the right edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: bottom
terminals.description: bottom is the vertical coordinate of the bottom edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: color(black)
terminals.description: color is the color of the pattern. In black and white, the VI uses white. You can wire a color box constant to this input.

terminals.name: new picture
terminals.description: Functions 2068 ni.com new picture is the picture that contains the new image. You can wire this output to any other picture input to add more drawing instructions to the picture. You also can wire this output to the Picture to Pixmap VI to obtain an image data cluster. You then can use the Graphics Formats VIs to save the image data to a file. You can wire this output to the Concatenate Strings function to concatenate the output from multiple Picture Functions VIs so that they draw on a single p

entries.name: Draw Round Rect
entries.description: Draws a rounded rectangle into a picture.

terminals.name: oval height
terminals.description: oval height is the height of an oval that defines the amount of curvature for the corners.

terminals.name: oval width
terminals.description: oval width is the width of an oval that defines the amount of curvature for the corners.

terminals.name: picture
terminals.description: picture is the picture to which you want to add the rectangle. The default is an empty picture.

terminals.name: rect
terminals.description: rect is a cluster that contains four numeric controls that describe the left, top, right, and bottom coordinates of the outer edges of a rectangle that circumscribes the rounded rectangle. Horizontal coordinates increase to the right, and vertical coordinates increase to the bottom. Functions

entries.name: Draw Oval
entries.description: Draws an oval in the rectangle you specify, optionally filling the interior. Functions © National Instruments 2071

terminals.name: picture
terminals.description: picture is the picture to which you want to add the oval. The default is an empty picture.

terminals.name: rect
terminals.description: rect is a cluster that contains four numeric controls that describe the left, top, right, and bottom coordinates of the outer edges of the rectangle. Horizontal coordinates increase to the right, and vertical coordinates increase to the bottom.

terminals.name: left
terminals.description: left is the horizontal coordinate of the left edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: top
terminals.description: top is the vertical coordinate of the top edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: right
terminals.description: right is the horizontal coordinate of the right edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: bottom
terminals.description: bottom is the vertical coordinate of the bottom edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: fill?(F)
terminals.description: fill? specifies whether to fill the interior. If FALSE (default), the VI draws only the outlines.

terminals.name: color(black)
terminals.description: color is the color of the lines. Functions 2072 ni.com If the interior is filled, the VI uses this color for the interior. The default is black. You can wire a color box constant to this input.

terminals.name: pen
terminals.description: pen sets the width and style of the pen the VI uses to draw the picture.

terminals.name: Width
terminals.description: Width sets the width in pixels of the pen the VI uses to draw the picture.

terminals.name: Style
terminals.description: Style sets the style of the line the VI uses to draw the picture. 0 Solid (default) 1 Dash 2 Dot 3 DashDot 4 DashDotDot

terminals.name: new picture
terminals.description: new picture is the picture that contains the new image. You can wire this output to any other picture input to add more drawing instructions to the picture. You also can wire this output to the Picture to Pixmap VI to obtain an image data cluster. You then can use the Graphics Formats VIs to save the image data to a file. You can wire this output to the Concatenate Strings function to concatenate the output from multiple Picture Functions VIs so that they draw on a single picture control.

entries.name: Draw Arc
entries.description: Draws an arc in a picture. Functions © National Instruments 2073

terminals.name: arc size[degrees]
terminals.description: arc size determines the degree value of the circle you want to draw. This value can range from -360 to 360. For example, a value of 360 creates a complete circle, and a value of 180 creates a semicircle.

terminals.name: start angle[degrees]
terminals.description: start angle determines where the arc begins.

terminals.name: picture
terminals.description: picture is the picture to which you want to add the arc. The default is an empty picture.

terminals.name: rect
terminals.description: rect is a cluster that contains four numeric controls that describe the left, top, right, and bottom coordinates of the outer edges of the rectangle. Horizontal coordinates increase to the right, and vertical coordinates increase to the bottom.

terminals.name: left
terminals.description: left is the horizontal coordinate of the left edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: top
terminals.description: top is the vertical coordinate of the top edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: right
terminals.description: right is the horizontal coordinate of the right edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: bottom
terminals.description: Functions 2074 ni.com bottom is the vertical coordinate of the bottom edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: fill?(F)
terminals.description: fill? specifies whether to fill the rectangle. If FALSE (default), the VI draws only the arc component and does not draw interior bounds of the wedge.

terminals.name: color(black)
terminals.description: color is the color of the lines. If the interior is filled, the VI uses this color for the interior. The default is black. You can wire a color box constant to this input.

terminals.name: pen
terminals.description: pen sets the width and style of the pen the VI uses to draw the picture.

terminals.name: Width
terminals.description: Width sets the width in pixels of the pen the VI uses to draw the picture.

terminals.name: Style
terminals.description: Style sets the style of the line the VI uses to draw the picture. 0 Solid (default) 1 Dash 2 Dot 3 DashDot 4 DashDotDot

terminals.name: new picture
terminals.description: new picture is the picture that contains the new image. You can wire this output to any other picture input to add more drawing instructions to the picture. Functions

entries.name: Draw Text at Point
entries.description: Draws a string into a picture. This VI automatically calculates the bounding rectangle of the text and positions the text with respect to the point you specify. Functions 2076 ni.com

terminals.name: [user-specified font]
terminals.description: [user-specified font] specifies the specific font characteristics for the text to draw. The VI ignores this input unless desired font is User-specified Font.

terminals.name: Font Name:
terminals.description: Font Name specifies the font to use for the text. If you misspell the font name or specify a font that is not installed on the computer, the operating system selects a font.

terminals.name: Size
terminals.description: Size specifies the size of the font in points.

terminals.name: Strikeout?
terminals.description: Strikeout? specifies if the text appears in strikeout. If Strikeout? is TRUE, the text appears in strikeout.

terminals.name: Italic?
terminals.description: Italic? specifies if the text appears in italics. If Italic? is TRUE, the text appears in italics.

terminals.name: Underline?
terminals.description: Underline? specifies if the text is underlined. If Underline? is TRUE, the text appears underlined.

terminals.name: Outline?
terminals.description: Outline? specifies if the text is outlined. If Outline? is TRUE, the text appears outlined.

terminals.name: Shadow?
terminals.description: Shadow? specifies if the text has a shadow. If Shadow? is TRUE, the text appears in shadow.

terminals.name: Bold?
terminals.description: Functions

entries.name: Draw Text in Rect
entries.description: Draws a string into a picture. If the string is larger than the rectangle you specify, the VI clips, or crops, the string to the area of the rectangle.

terminals.name: [user-specified font]
terminals.description: [user-specified font] specifies the specific font characteristics for the text to draw. The VI ignores this input unless desired font is User-specified Font.

terminals.name: Font Name:
terminals.description: Font Name specifies the font to use for the text. If you misspell the font name or specify a font that is not installed on the computer, the operating system selects a font.

terminals.name: Size
terminals.description: Functions 2084 ni.com Size specifies the size of the font in points.

terminals.name: Strikeout?
terminals.description: Strikeout? specifies if the text appears in strikeout. If Strikeout? is TRUE, the text appears in strikeout.

terminals.name: Italic?
terminals.description: Italic? specifies if the text appears in italics. If Italic? is TRUE, the text appears in italics.

terminals.name: Underline?
terminals.description: Underline? specifies if the text is underlined. If Underline? is TRUE, the text appears underlined.

terminals.name: Outline?
terminals.description: Outline? specifies if the text is outlined. If Outline? is TRUE, the text appears outlined.

terminals.name: Shadow?
terminals.description: Shadow? specifies if the text has a shadow. If Shadow? is TRUE, the text appears in shadow.

terminals.name: Bold?
terminals.description: Bold? specifies if the text is bold. If Bold? is TRUE, the text appears bold.

terminals.name: desired font(Application)
terminals.description: desired font specifies the text font. 0 User-specified Font 1 Application Font (default) 2 System Font 3 Dialog Font

terminals.name: picture
terminals.description: picture is the picture to which you want to add the text string. The default is an empty picture. Functions

entries.name: Draw Circle by Radius
entries.description: Draws a circle based on a specified radius and center point.

terminals.name: arc size(360)
terminals.description: arc size determines the degree value of the circle you want to draw. This value can range from -360 to 360. For example, a value of 360 creates a complete circle, and a value of 180 creates a semicircle. Functions © National Instruments 2087

terminals.name: start angle(0)
terminals.description: start angle determines where the arc begins.

terminals.name: picture
terminals.description: picture is the picture to which you want to add the circle. The default is an empty picture.

terminals.name: radius
terminals.description: radius determines the size of the circle, in pixels. If, for example, this value is 40, the radius of the resulting circle is 40 pixels.

terminals.name: pixel center
terminals.description: pixel center contains the coordinates around which the VI draws the circle.

terminals.name: horizontal
terminals.description: horizontal is the pixel center of the circle relative to the x-coordinate of the current origin.

terminals.name: vertical
terminals.description: vertical is the pixel center of the circle relative to the y-coordinate of the current origin.

terminals.name: fill?(F)
terminals.description: fill? specifies whether to fill the interior. If FALSE (default), the VI draws only the outlines.

terminals.name: color(black)
terminals.description: color sets the pen color to use when drawing the oval. If unwired, the VI uses the last color specified as the pen color. You can wire a color box constant to this input.

terminals.name: pen
terminals.description: pen sets the width and style of the pen the VI uses to draw the picture. Functions 2088 ni.com

terminals.name: Width
terminals.description: Width sets the width in pixels of the pen the VI uses to draw the picture.

terminals.name: Style
terminals.description: Style sets the style of the line the VI uses to draw the picture. 0 Solid (default) 1 Dash 2 Dot 3 DashDot 4 DashDotDot

terminals.name: new picture
terminals.description: new picture is the picture that contains the new image. You can wire this output to any other picture input to add more drawing instructions to the picture. You also can wire this output to the Picture to Pixmap VI to obtain an image data cluster. You then can use the Graphics Formats VIs to save the image data to a file. entries.name: Hilite Color
entries.description: Returns two new colors well matched to the input color for highlight and shadow coloration, based on the input color and a percentage you specify. You can use the highlight colors to create three-dimensionally shaded objects.

terminals.name: Color In
terminals.description: Color In is the input color on which to base the returned highlight and shadow colors. You can wire a color box constant to this input.

terminals.name: Percentage(30)
terminals.description: Percentage determines the degree of highlighting and shadow in the new colors. The default is 30 percent.

terminals.name: Hilite
terminals.description: Hilite is the new color matched as a highlight to the input color.

terminals.name: Shadow
terminals.description: Shadow is the new color matched as a shadow to the input color. Functions 2090 ni.com

entries.name: Draw Unflattened Pixmap
entries.description: Converts a pixmap into a picture so you can use other Picture Functions VIs to append drawing instructions to the image. The data type you wire to the data input determines the polymorphic instance to use. If you want to convert a 4-bit or 8-bit pixmap, you must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use. • Draw True-Color Pixmap VI • Draw 1-Bit Pixmap VI • Draw 4-Bit Pixmap VI • Draw 8-Bit Pixmap VI Draw True-Color Pixmap VI Draw True-Color Pixmap VI Converts a pixmap into a pictu

terminals.name: picture
terminals.description: picture is the picture to which you want to add the pixmap. The default is an empty picture.

terminals.name: top left point(0, 0)
terminals.description: top left point specifies in coordinates where to place the top-left corner of the image in the new picture. Functions © National Instruments 2091

terminals.name: data (RGB format)
terminals.description: data is a 2D array of 32-bit unsigned integers that describes the color of each pixel in the image in raster order. Each pixel has three bytes to describe its color. The first byte for each pixel describes the red value, the second byte describes the green value, and the third byte describes the blue value.

terminals.name: mask
terminals.description: mask is an array of bytes in which each bit describes mask information for a pixel. The first byte describes the first eight pixels, the second byte describes the next eight pixels, and so on. If a bit is zero, LabVIEW draws the corresponding pixel as transparent. If the array is empty, LabVIEW draws all pixels without transparency. If the array does not contain a bit for each pixel in the image, LabVIEW draws any pixels missing from the array without transparency.

terminals.name: new picture
terminals.description: new picture is the picture that contains the new image. You can wire this output to any other picture input to add more drawing instructions to the picture.

entries.name: Draw Flattened Pixmap
entries.description: Draws a 1-, 4-, or 8-bit pixmap or a 24-bit RGB pixmap into a picture. This VI takes a 1D array of bytes as input and assumes the user completes all packing and padding.

terminals.name: picture
terminals.description: picture is the picture to which you want to add the array of data. The default is an empty picture.

terminals.name: image data
terminals.description: image data describes the image you want to draw or manipulate.

terminals.name: image type
terminals.description: image type is reserved for future use.

terminals.name: image depth
terminals.description: image depth specifies the color depth of the image, which is the number of bits to use to Functions © National Instruments 2097 describe the color of each pixel in the image. Valid values include 1, 4, 8, and 24 bits per pixel. image depth affects how LabVIEW interprets the values of image and colors.

terminals.name: image
terminals.description: image is an array of bytes that describes the color of each pixel in the image in raster order. The value of image depth determines how LabVIEW interprets the value of this input. If image depth is 24, each pixel has three bytes to describe its color. The first byte for each pixel describes the red value, the second byte describes the green value, and the third byte describes the blue value. If image depth is 8, each pixel has one byte to describe its color. The value of each byte corresponds to

terminals.name: mask
terminals.description: mask is an array of bytes in which each bit describes mask information for a pixel. The first byte describes the first eight pixels, the second byte describes the next eight pixels, and so on. If a bit is zero, LabVIEW draws the corresponding pixel as transparent. If the array is empty, LabVIEW draws all pixels without transparency. If the array does not contain a bit for each pixel in the image, LabVIEW draws any pixels missing from the array without transparency.

terminals.name: colors
terminals.description: colors is an array of RGB color values that correspond to the values in image. The value of image depth determines how LabVIEW interprets the value of this input. colors stores 32-bit RGB values where the most-significant byte is zero, followed in order by red, green, and blue values. Valid values include 0 through 255. Functions 2098 ni.com If image depth is 24, LabVIEW ignores this input. If image depth is 8, the array can have 256 elements. If image depth is 4, the array can have 16 elements.

terminals.name: Rectangle
terminals.description: Rectangle is a cluster that contains coordinates that describe the bounding rectangle in which you want to draw the image. The VI clips the image to the width and height of the rectangle. The bottom and right edges of the rectangle bounds do not contain image pixels. Horizontal coordinates increase to the right, and vertical coordinates increase to the bottom.

terminals.name: left
terminals.description: left is the horizontal coordinate of the left edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: top
terminals.description: top is the vertical coordinate of the top edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: right
terminals.description: right is the horizontal coordinate of the right edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: bottom
terminals.description: bottom is the vertical coordinate of the bottom edge of the rectangle.

terminals.name: new picture
terminals.description: new picture is the picture that contains the new image. You can wire this output to any other picture input to add more drawing instructions to the picture. Functions © National Instruments 2099

entries.name: Create Mask
entries.description: Applies a mask to an image and returns the mask information in the mask element of image data. This VI is useful if you want to make a certain color in an image transparent before writing the image to a 2D picture control.

terminals.name: image data
terminals.description: image data returns information about the image so you can use the Draw Flattened Pixmap VI to draw it as a picture.

terminals.name: image type
terminals.description: image type is reserved for future use.

terminals.name: image depth

Valid values include 1, 4, 8, and 24 bits per pixel. image depth affects how LabVIEW interprets the values of image and colors.

The first byte for each pixel describes the red value, the second byte describes the green value, and the third byte describes the blue value. If image depth is 8, each pixel has one byte to describe its color. The value of each byte corresponds t

The first byte describes the first eight pixels, the second byte describes the next eight pixels, and so on. If a bit is zero, LabVIEW draws the corresponding pixel as transparent. If the array is empty, LabVIEW draws all pixels without transparency. If the array does not contain a bit for each pixel in the image, LabVIEW draws any pixels missing from the array without transparency.
